sections.heading: HPV and Oncoproteins
sections.content: The molecular pathogenesis of penile cancer is closely linked to human papillomavirus (HPV) infection, detected in 45% to 80% of cases, particularly HPV types 16, 6, and 18. HPV oncoproteins E6 and E7 inactivate tumor suppressors p53 and pRb, respectively, leading to genomic instability and carcinogenesis. HPV-positive tumors often show better prognosis, possibly due to immune recognition.

sections.heading: p53 Pathway Alterations
sections.content: In HPV-independent tumors, alterations in the p53 pathway are common, often due to mutations in TP53.

sections.heading: Other Molecular Events
sections.content: Other molecular events include overexpression of EGFR, cyclin D1, and activation of the PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway.

sections.heading: Chromosomal Abnormalities
sections.content: Chromosomal abnormalities such as gains at 8q and losses at 3p, 8p, and 13q have been reported.

sections.heading: Multigene Panel Testing
sections.content: Multigene panel testing (MGPT) in Clinical Laboratory Improvement Amendments (CLIA)-approved laboratories can identify rare and actionable mutations and fusions, which may guide therapy in advanced disease. [6,7,8]
